title: Relativistic Effects in Spin Correlations Induced by QED Scattering and Wigner Rotations
authors: Juan D. Fonseca, B. Hiller, I. G. da Paz, M. Sampaio
abstract: We study the relativistic nature of the interactions that, at tree level, generate spin correlations between two electrons in Møller scattering, as well as in an extended process involving a witness particle $C$. The corresponding processes, $e^{-}e^{-}\rightarrow e^{-}e^{-}$ and $e^{-}e^{-}C\rightarrow e^{-}e^{-}C$, are analyzed both in the center-of-mass frame and, for the former process, in a Lorentz-boosted frame where Wigner rotations arise. It is found that, through a nonrelativistic approximation of the scattering amplitudes, dipole-dipole and current-dipole interactions are responsible for the emergence of these correlations. This is evidenced by the variation of the von Neumann entropy of one electron for initially separable states, and of $C$ for an initially prepared three-particle entangled W-state. In Wigner rotations, the invariance of entropy under local unitary transformations is maintained at the expense of the emergence of quantum coherence in the density matrix at large rapidities. As a consequence, the final states of both particles are evaluated and shown to encode information about the scattering process through their spin expectation values. This framework is then used to comment on the correlations in the inelastic process $e^{-}e^{+}\rightarrowμ^{-}μ^{+}$, for which some research has reported differing results.
summary: This theoretical paper analyzes how spin correlations develop during electron scattering processes by incorporating full relativistic effects, specifically Wigner rotations. Using entanglement entropy as a diagnostic tool, the authors show that while total entropy remains invariant under boosts, local spin observables are frame-dependent. The work provides a detailed framework for understanding quantum correlations in high-energy particle physics.
main_problem: The study investigates the relativistic effects, particularly those arising from Wigner rotations, that generate spin correlations between electrons during QED scattering processes like Møller scattering.
main_result: Dipole-dipole and current-dipole interactions are responsible for generating these spin correlations; while total entropy is Lorentz invariant, local observables like spin expectation values are frame-dependent.
method: The authors analyze scattering amplitudes using nonrelativistic approximations and calculate quantum information measures such as the von Neumann entropy of reduced density matrices in different reference frames.
model_or_system: The system involves electron-electron ($e^-e^-$) or three-particle ($e^-e^-C$) interactions governed by Quantum Electrodynamics (QED). The analysis is performed using scattering theory formalism, including the use of density matrices and Lorentz transformations to account for relativistic kinematics.
key_observables: Spin correlations, von Neumann entropy ($S_{	ext{Neumann}}$), and spin expectation values ($\langle S_{x,y,z} angle$) of the outgoing particles.
important_parameters: Collision energy/rapidities ($\eta, \omega$), scattering angles ($	heta, \phi$), and the presence of a witness particle C.
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to tree-level contributions in perturbation theory, omitting higher orders in the coupling constant. The initial states are analyzed as both separable and entangled (W-state).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate collision diagrams for $e^-e^-$ scattering, Feynman diagrams for Møller scattering, and plots showing the variation of von Neumann entropy and spin expectation values versus collision energy or rapidity for different interaction types.
paper_structure: The paper proceeds by analyzing the process in the center-of-mass frame, deriving amplitudes using nonrelativistic approximations, examining entanglement measures (entropy) under Lorentz boosts involving Wigner rotations, and finally applying this framework to comment on other processes like $e^-e^+ 	o \mu^-\mu^+$
why_interesting: The rigorous treatment of spin correlations in relativistic scattering provides a deep link between fundamental QFT symmetries (Lorentz invariance) and quantum information measures (entropy), which is relevant for understanding decoherence mechanisms in high-energy physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
